document_metadata.title: Hidden Architecture Patterns — Reverse-Engineered from Configuration Behavior and API Surface (Refined Review)
document_metadata.purpose: Refined inferences and implementation guidance for three hidden architectural subsystems inferred from API/config behavior, with deeper quality-management grounding and single-tenant right-sizing for an automotive QMS target system.
document_metadata.method: Interpretive reverse-engineering with explicit confidence ratings, alternative explanations, and failure-mode analysis; mapped to NCR/CAPA/SCAR/8D domain constraints.

pattern_1_guided_process_orchestration.summary: A multi-step orchestration experience that bundles related forms/steps into a sequenced UX, while coordinating per-step workflows/approvals and tracking overall completion as a unit via a monitoring/join mechanism.

facts: A guided process bundles multiple forms into a single step-by-step experience
Each form in the bundle can have its own workflow, with a default workflow for unassigned forms
Forms are presented in a configurable order (move up/down)
Individual forms can be marked as skippable
The guided process has three modes: Read Only, Edit, and New
A system-generated monitoring workflow tracks completion of ALL forms as a unit
States are tracked: Open (in progress), Completed (all forms processed)
Each form's submission and processing status is logged in the monitoring workflow history
A summary dialog shows: guided process name, submission date, total forms, forms completed, forms skipped
Guided processes must be published before becoming active
Once published, the reference code becomes immutable
Properties: name (required), reference_code (required, immutable after publish), description, mode, effective_from, effective_to

orchestration_layer_architecture.inference: Guided process is a logical orchestration layer that coordinates multiple per-step workflow executions and tracks overall completion as a unit.
evidence: Each form can have its own workflow (multiple independent workflow executions)
There is a separate monitoring mechanism tracking all forms as a unit
Guided process has lifecycle and effective dating separate from child workflows
alternative_explanations: The guided process may be implemented as a generated workflow definition within the same workflow engine (template pattern), rather than a separate runtime service.
orchestration_layer_architecture.deduced_architecture: Two-tier coordination model: a parent instance tracks step completion/skip state and acts as a join/barrier, while each step runs its own workflow/approval logic.

state_machine_per_step.inference: Each guided-process step requires a state model that distinguishes user-entry completion, approval pending, commit completion, skip, and return-for-changes.
evidence: Monitoring history logs both submission and processing status
Completed vs skipped counts are tracked explicitly
notes: A 4-state model (NotStarted/InProgress/Completed/Skipped) is insufficient for approval-driven processes because it collapses Submitted vs Approved/Committed and cannot represent ReturnedForChanges.

dependency_and_ordering_model.inference: Presentation order and enforcement dependencies are separate concepts: order controls UX; prerequisites control whether a step can be submitted/committed and whether the overall process can complete.
evidence: Move up/down ordering implies a sequential UX
Skippable flags imply not all steps are required to complete
Overall 'Completed' implies required-steps gating exists somewhere

resolved_open_question.question: Can a non-skippable form block progression to the next form?
resolved_open_question.answer: For dependency-heavy processes (e.g., 8D), enforce prerequisites at submission/commit boundaries rather than blocking navigation. A non-skippable (or prerequisite) step must block (a) submitting downstream steps that depend on it and (b) marking the overall guided process completed.

recommended_model.completion_gate: All required steps must be Committed or explicitly Skipped with reason/authorization
recommended_model.navigation: Allow viewing later steps, but block submit/commit if prerequisites unmet

mode_architecture.inference: Mode is a guided-process-level parameter that establishes the shared data context for all steps (create new root vs edit existing vs read-only).
evidence: Mode is set at guided process definition level, not per form
New vs Edit implies different root-entity behavior
alternative_explanations: Some guided processes may mix create-on-first-step with edit-on-subsequent-steps while still being labeled 'New'.
mode_architecture.deduced_architecture: Mode establishes a root entity identity context passed through steps; steps may create or update subordinate records attached to the root.

monitoring_workflow_architecture.inference: The monitoring mechanism implements a join/barrier over child step outcomes and records a human-auditable progress history.
evidence: System-generated monitoring workflow
Tracks completion of ALL forms as a unit
Logs per-form submission/processing status
implementation_possibilities: Event-driven subscription to child workflow terminal events
Synchronous updates to a progress table at each child workflow terminal node
monitoring_workflow_architecture.key_property: Barrier/join semantics: overall completes only when required steps are resolved (Committed/Skipped).

blind_spots_and_failure_modes: Returned-for-changes loops must be explicit; otherwise approvals can't push work back without corrupting history
Abandoned guided processes require reminders/escalations and explicit cancel/close behavior
Step ownership and contributor roles must be modeled; not every step is edited by the same user
Definition/version pinning is mandatory so in-flight investigations are stable even if step definitions evolve

mapping.mode: New (create EightDReport) or Edit (revise in-flight report)

steps.commit_timing_note: Often requires presave/provisional commit with separate approval trail.

deliverables: Action plan linked to root causes

mapping.per_step_approval: Each step can route to its own approval chain; rejected steps must move to ReturnedForChanges with resubmission producing a new immutable staged payload.
mapping.monitoring: Overall progress derived from per-step state and used to enforce closure guards and SLA escalation.

simplify: Replace complex system-generated monitoring workflows with computed progress views/tables if desired
Treat 'publish' as 'active version' without multi-tenant governance overhead
drop: Overly generic configurability where only one company uses the system

pattern_2_policy_resolution_engine.summary: An effective-dated, conflict-resolving policy selection pattern that determines which configured rule set applies for a given entity/context at a given point in time, using specificity and priority rules.

explicit_policy_types_documented.assignment: One per role (required, auto-populated with default on role creation)
explicit_policy_types_documented.resolution: Multiple policies coexist. Each has a priority number. Platform sorts by strength (highest priority first) and applies the strongest matching policy.

explicit_policy_types_documented.assignment: Referenced as a search parameter on the Employee XRefCodes endpoint. Supports ContextDate (point-in-time resolution).
explicit_policy_types_documented.evidence: payPolicyXRefCode search parameter with note: 'Use a ContextDate value to search for employees with a given pay policy as of a point in time.'
explicit_policy_types_documented.source: Web Services Guide → Employee search parameters

explicit_policy_types_documented.assignment: Referenced identically to Pay Policy — searchable, ContextDate-aware.
explicit_policy_types_documented.source: Web Services Guide → Employee search parameters

explicit_policy_types_documented.assignment: Listed as a subordinate resource of Employee (read and write). Multiple assignments possible per employee.
explicit_policy_types_documented.source: Web Services Guide → Employee subordinate resources

explicit_policy_types_documented.assignment: Implied by the raw punch data description: 'Raw clock entry data refers to employee clock entry records BEFORE any manager edits or time entry policy rules are applied.'
Implied by balance transaction description: 'balances incremented by entitlement policies.'
explicit_policy_types_documented.source: Web Services Guide → Employee Balance Transactions

common_characteristics: All policy types are referenced by XRefCode (tenant-configurable identifiers)
All policy assignments support effective dating (ContextDate resolution)
Multiple policies of the same type can coexist in the system

generalized_policy_resolution_architectu.inference: There is a shared policy model (effective-dated assignments + identifiers) but resolution may vary by domain; however, all require deterministic conflict resolution.
evidence: ContextDate behavior appears consistent
Password policy explicitly documents priority-based resolution
risks: Assuming identical resolution logic across policy types may be incorrect; some domains may use specificity-first, others priority-first, and some may be additive.

recommended_minimum.determinism: Always define explicit tie-breakers to prevent ambiguous/flickering outcomes

policy_as_rule_container.inference: Policies are containers of rules; policy resolution selects the container, and domain logic executes the contained rules.
evidence: Password policy bundles multiple rule types
Time entry and entitlement descriptions imply multiple rules per policy

assignment_chain_architecture.inference: Policy applicability is frequently inherited (role/org/work assignment) but should allow explicit exceptions/overrides.
evidence: Password policy via role inheritance
Other policies likely derive from work assignment context
assignment_chain_architecture.recommended_design_note: Model exception overrides explicitly rather than burying them in procedural logic.

raw_to_processed_pipeline_as_policy_exec.inference: Raw→processed transformations are policy-driven pipelines that preserve rejected inputs for review and audit.
evidence: Raw punches exist before rules applied; processed punches exist after
Rejected vs processed states exist

blind_spots_and_failure_modes: Some policy outcomes are additive (multiple recipients/escalations), not single-winner
No-match and tie behaviors must be defined (default vs error)
Policy version/pin requirements for audit: record which policy was used at decision time
Performance requires indexes aligned to effective dating and priority

recommended_policy_types: SCAR trigger policy (repeat issues, supplier score thresholds)
relevance_to_quality_management.avoid_overengineering: Do not build a general-purpose rule DSL; implement a small set of consistent effective-dated resolver procs over strongly typed policy tables.

simplify: No tenant dimension; fewer policy dimensions
drop: Tenant-configurable schema (EAV) and arbitrary expression-language engines

pattern_3_data_staging_and_edit_mode_arc.summary: A staged change architecture where form submissions create immutable pending payloads that flow through approvals and only become committed state when an explicit commit/apply step runs; validate-only runs through the same rules without committing.

explicit_evidence: Process node commits staged data; data is not committed on submission
Presave indicates pre-approval commit is supported
Workflow validation sends full XML payload because data is not committed
Requester cannot alter the XML payload after submission
Expression engine supports staged-vs-committed comparison (HasCurrentPrimaryChanged) and read-through fallback (LoadIfEmpty)
UpdateStatus metadata exists for staged list operations

staging_layer_architecture.inference: A staging store exists that holds immutable submitted payloads linked to workflow/approval execution, with commit/apply moving changes into committed tables.
deduced_properties: Discardable or preserved depending on rejection/audit requirements

presave_vs_approved_commit_patterns.inference: Presave commits data early with a pending/provisional marker; Approved commits only after approval. Rejection of presaved changes must rescind/void rather than attempt silent rollback.
presave_vs_approved_commit_patterns.risk_note: Presave creates downstream operational side effects; compensation must be explicit and auditable.

partial_entity_staging_and_edit_modes.inference: Edit modes support field-group scoped staging with distinct approval flows; concurrent pending edits are possible only with explicit conflict rules.
missing_from_original: Apply-time revalidation against current committed state
Conflict detection based on base state/row version or base field values

multi_party_staging.inference: Cross-party processes use party-specific pending states and commit barriers requiring both parties to complete; staged payloads may be per-party.

blind_spots_and_failure_modes: Approval apply must revalidate guards/policies due to time delay drift
Concurrency conflicts must be detected at apply time (stale state / row version / base-field overlap)
Apply must be idempotent under retries
Presave rejection must create explicit rescind/supersede trail and follow-up tasks when physical actions may have occurred

applications: NCR investigation edits staged for days with return-for-changes loops
Containment actions need provisional visibility (presave) with rescind-not-revert compensation
SCAR requires per-party payload + status tracking
Edit modes map to field-group specific approvals (severity vs containment)
relevance_to_quality_management.critical_concurrency_answer: When two pending edits coexist, apply must check whether the assumptions used at staging time still hold; if entity state or targeted fields changed, block apply and require resubmission.

do_not_simplify_away: Concurrency handling and apply-time revalidation (single tenant still needs these)

how_the_three_patterns_interact.description: The combined system forms a controlled pipeline: guided process tracks multi-step progress; each step submits staged payloads; policies determine required steps/approvals/SLAs; workflow approvals gate apply/commit; apply revalidates against current state to prevent stale approvals.
pipeline: Guided Process Instance (step progress + prerequisites + overall completion gate)
-> Step Submission creates immutable staged payload (Pending change-set / workflow transaction)
-> Policy Resolution determines required steps, approval chains, SLA timers, escalation rules
-> Approval Engine routes to approvers; rejection returns for changes; timeouts expire and escalate
-> Apply/Commit runs in a transaction, revalidates state/policy, detects conflicts, then commits

missing_artifacts_in_original: Correlation IDs linking guided process instance, step instance, staged payload, approvals, and final commit
Explicit compensation rules for presave side effects
Explicit concurrency strategy (serialize vs field-group merge)
Partial completion handling (abandonment, policy drift, definition drift)

guided_process_failures: Step not submitted -> process remains Open; SLA reminders/escalations fire
Improper skip -> closure guard blocks closing parent record
staging_failures: Validation failure -> no pending approval created; remain InProgress
Apply conflict -> move to ReturnedForChanges with conflict reason
policy_failures: No policy match -> default or error depending on rule criticality
Ambiguous tie -> deterministic winner + warning or hard error for high-stakes policies
approval_failures: Stale approval -> block apply and require resubmission

compensation_and_rollback_flows.approved_mode: Reject/return discards staged payload from becoming committed; payload and decision remain auditable.
compensation_and_rollback_flows.presave_mode: Reject rescinds/voids provisional data; never silent rollback; create explicit follow-up tasks for real-world actions.

recommended_rules: Revalidate at apply time using current committed state
Detect conflicts via base state + row version and/or base-field overlap checks
If conflict, block apply and return for changes (do not auto-merge unless overlap rules are provably safe)
concurrency_and_conflict_resolution.partial_field_group_merge_note: Allow parallel edits only when change-set type has explicit base-field overlap detection; otherwise serialize per entity.

abandonment: Allow explicit cancel with reason; preserve history
policy_change_mid_staging: Re-resolve policy at apply time; if materially different, block apply and require resubmission
definition_change_mid_staging: Pin DefinitionVersion at instance creation; never hot-swap definitions on in-flight guided processes
